channel.title: NYT Book Reviews (Filtered)
channel.description: Actual book reviews from the New York Times, filtered from the noisy Books RSS feed.

item.title: ‘One Leg on Earth,’ by ‘Pemi Aguda
item.description: In “One Leg on Earth,” a young college grad’s idealistic move to Lagos turns into a nightmare.
item.author: Ainslie Hogarth

item.title: ‘Screen People,’ by Megan Garber
item.description: In “Screen People,” Megan Garber looks at how we all became famous for 15 minutes.
item.author: Madison Malone Kircher

item.title: ‘Revenge for the Sixties,’ by Peter S. Canellos; ‘Alito,’ by Mollie Hemingway
item.description: Two new biographies of the Supreme Court justice show how his career was propelled by a legal movement that coalesced to take down Roe v. Wade.
item.author: Mary Jo McConahay

item.title: ‘The Last Contract of Isako,’ by Fonda Lee
item.description: Fonda Lee’s “cyberpunk samurai in space” novel follows a sword-wielding warrior trying to finish one last job.
item.author: Amal El-Mohtar

item.title: ‘From Life Itself,’ by Suzy Hansen
item.description: In a new book, the journalist Suzy Hansen plumbs an Istanbul community for insights into Turkey’s hard-right turn.
item.author: Jennifer Szalai

item.title: ‘The Family Man,’ by James Lasdun
item.description: “The Family Man,” by the novelist and poet James Lasdun, brings a literary voice and elaborate detail to a case that gripped the nation.
item.author: Orlando Whitfield

item.title: ‘The Hill,’ by Harriet Clark
item.description: Partly inspired by her life, Harriet Clark’s “The Hill” portrays a young girl navigating between her beloved mother’s jail cell and the world outside.
item.author: Hermione Hoby

item.title: ‘Riverwork,’ by Lisa Robertson
item.description: “Riverwork,” by Lisa Robertson, considers the lost history of the Bièvre and the lives of working women once linked to it.
item.author: Kerry Howley

item.title: ‘List of All Possible Desires,’ by Dylan Landis
item.description: In a new novel told in interlinked stories, Dylan Landis revisits a dauntless family she has written about since 2009.
item.author: Joan Silber

item.title: ‘Prestige Drama,’ by Seamas O’Reilly
item.description: Séamas O’Reilly’s new novel is a boisterous sendup of “prestige” media and its distortion of Northern Ireland’s complex past.
item.author: Colin Barrett

item.title: ‘John of John,’ by Douglas Stuart
item.description: In the powerful and surprising “John of John,” Douglas Stuart sends a young art student back home to a family he thought he’d left behind.
item.author: Mark Harris

item.title: ‘The Things We Never Say,’ by Elizabeth Strout
item.description: “The Things We Never Say” leaves behind Crosby, Maine, for Massachusetts, where a middle-aged history teacher discovers a long-buried family secret.
item.author: Maggie Shipstead

item.title: ‘Ghost Stories,’ by Siri Hustvedt
item.description: In a new book, Siri Hustvedt recalls her life with the writer Paul Auster and the story of his illness.
item.author: Dwight Garner

item.title: ‘The Calamity Club,’ by Kathryn Stockett
item.description: Kathryn Stockett’s prodigious second novel, “The Calamity Club,” brings together an unlikely group of spinsters, sex workers and orphans in Depression-era Mississippi.
item.author: Lauren Christensen

item.title: ‘True Crime,’ by Patricia Cornwell
item.description: Her new memoir, “True Crime,” traces how she survived a Southern Gothic upbringing to emerge as one of the world’s most famous thriller writers.
item.author: Alexandra Jacobs

item.title: ‘Backtalker,’ by Kimberlé Williams Crenshaw
item.description: In her memoir “Backtalker,” Kimberlé Williams Crenshaw shows how personal trauma spurred her influential and controversial ideas about race and gender.
item.author: Colin Grant

item.title: ‘The Successor,’ by Mikhail Fishman
item.description: In “The Successor,” the exiled journalist Mikhail Fishman tells the story of a charming Russian politician who might have made his country into a liberal democracy.
item.author: David Kortava

item.title: ‘Chernobyl, Life, and Other Disasters,’ by Yevgenia Nayberg
item.description: Eleven-year-old Genya plays the pretending game as she crams for an art school entrance exam in Chernobyl’s wake.
item.author: Elena Gorokhova

item.title: ‘Cave Mountain,’ by Benjamin Hale
item.description: Benjamin Hale’s book “Cave Mountain” connects the brief disappearance of his cousin in 2001 to a grisly true-crime story in 1978.
item.author: Jonathan Russell Clark

item.title: ‘Prophecy,’ by Carissa Véliz
item.description: In “Prophecy,” Carissa Véliz explores how generative A.I. relies on prediction, enriching Big Tech while making the rest of us less safe.
item.author: Jennifer Szalai

item.title: “Japanese Gothic,” by Kylie Lee Baker
item.description: In “Japanese Gothic,” a 21st-century college student and a 19th-century samurai find themselves occupying the same house.
item.author: Emily C. Hughes

item.title: ‘Aside From My Heart, All Is Well,’ by Héctor Abad
item.description: The worldly men of the cloth in Héctor Abad’s new novel find divinity both inside and outside the church.
item.author: Miguel Salazar

item.title: ‘Project Maven,’ by Katrina Manson
item.description: In “Project Maven,” Katrina Manson shows us how close we are to artificial intelligence picking targets and dropping bombs without human input.
item.author: Fred Kaplan

item.title: ‘The Hothouse’ and ‘Death in Rome,’ by Wolfgang Koeppen
item.description: The German writer Wolfgang Koeppen’s postwar trilogy crackles with life and unsparing details of a broken society.
item.author: Dustin Illingworth

item.title: ‘The Rolling Stones,’ by Bob Spitz
item.description: The music journalist Bob Spitz, a keeper of numerous rock ’n’ roll flames, has turned out a colorful and authoritative new take on a much-documented band.
item.author: Leah Greenblatt

item.title: ‘Small Town Girls,’ by Jayne Anne Phillips
item.description: A new book by Jayne Anne Phillips, a Pulitzer-winning novelist, recalling her childhood is a bittersweet triumph.
item.author: Sigrid Nunez

item.title: ‘Ghost Town,’ by Tom Perrotta
item.description: A middle-aged novelist sifts through memories of growing up in New Jersey in Tom Perrotta’s frustratingly formulaic book.
item.author: Alexandra Jacobs

item.title: ‘Like, Follow, Subscribe’ by Fortesa Latifi
item.description: In her engaging, sympathetic book “Like, Follow, Subscribe,” the journalist Fortesa Latifi digs into growing up in the spotlight.
item.author: Natasha Singer

item.title: ‘Homesick for a World Unknown,’ by Miriam Horn
item.description: In her engaging, lyrical “Homesick for a World Unknown,” Miriam Horn tells the story of the famed naturalist George Schaller.
item.author: Gary Rosen

item.title: ‘If This Be Magic,’ by Daniel Hahn
item.description: The translator Daniel Hahn makes the case that Shakespeare can be appreciated “even if we don’t hear a single one of his words.”
item.author: John McWhorter

item.title: ‘The Radiant Dark,’ by Alexandra Oliva
item.description: In “The Radiant Dark,” life is upended after humanity receives a signal from a distant planet. But extraterrestrial contact takes a back seat to more earthly problems.
item.author: Jen Doll

item.title: ‘Make Believe: On Telling Stories to Children,’ by Mac Barnett
item.description: In his chatty, compulsively readable first book for adults, Mac Barnett champions his career choice and urges our culture to hold kids in higher esteem.
item.author: Gregory Maguire

item.title: ‘Israel: What Went Wrong?,’ by Omer Bartov
item.description: In “Israel: What Went Wrong?,” Omer Bartov charts how a nation founded in the wake of trauma abandoned the emancipatory impulse of its origins.
item.author: Jennifer Szalai

item.title: ‘Permanence,’ by Sophie Mackintosh
item.description: In Sophie Mackintosh’s novel “Permanence,” cheating couples find themselves in an alternate world free of complication — and missing the mess.
item.author: Katie Kitamura

item.title: ‘How It Feels to Be Alive,’ by Megan O’Grady
item.description: “How It Feels to Be Alive,” by Megan O’Grady, blends criticism with personal history to explore how and why art affects us.
item.author: Charles Finch

item.title: ‘The Palm House,’ by Gwendoline Riley
item.description: In “The Palm House,” Gwendoline Riley offers understated yet cleareyed observations of human behavior — this time about middle-aged Londoners struggling to stay relevant.
item.author: Christian Lorentzen

item.title: ‘Jan Morris: A Life,’ by Sara Wheeler
item.description: A new biography of Jan Morris shows why the journalist, world traveler, historian and essayist was far more than a trailblazer.
item.author: Dwight Garner

item.title: ‘This Vast Enterprise,’ by Craig Fehrman.
item.description: In “This Vast Enterprise,” Craig Fehrman refreshes a familiar story with a rich chorus of voices.
item.author: Andrea Wulf

item.title: ‘When We See You Again,’ by Rachel Goldberg-Polin
item.description: Rachel Goldberg-Polin’s precise and devastating memoir chronicles the 328 days her son was held hostage in Gaza, and what came after.
item.author: Alexandra Jacobs

item.title: ‘How to Be a Dissident,’ by Gal Beckerman
item.description: In “How to Be a Dissident,” Gal Beckerman offers an inspiring tour of famous renegades with lessons for the rabble-rousers of today.
item.author: Astra Taylor

item.title: ‘EXTRA SAUCE’ by Zahra Tangorra, ‘ON EATING' by Alicia Kennedy
item.description: Both authors share uncanny similarities of upbringing. But their culinary paths diverged sharply.
item.author: Jennifer Reese

item.title: ‘Dear Monica Lewinsky,’ by Julia Langbein
item.description: Julia Langbein’s novel considers the legions of women whose lives have been forever marred by compromising early relationships.
item.author: Zoe Dubno

item.title: ‘The Violence,’ by Adriana E. Ramírez
item.description: Through accounts of relatives and direct witnesses, Adriana E. Ramírez examines a pivotal, and brutal, period of history.
item.author: Annie Correal

item.title: ‘Muskism,’ by Quinn Slobodian and Ben Tarnoff
item.description: In a new book, Quinn Slobodian and Ben Tarnoff argue that Elon Musk’s disruptive approach to business is transforming both politics and the economy.
item.author: Jennifer Szalai

item.title: ‘Rasputin’ by Antony Beevor
item.description: In “Rasputin,” the biographer Antony Beevor delves into the mysterious life of the last czarina’s mystic adviser.
item.author: Sophie Pinkham

item.title: ‘Into the Wood Chipper,’ by Nicholas Enrich
item.description: Nicholas Enrich’s tell-all memoir, “Into the Wood Chipper,” has advice for others caught between their conscience and their government.
item.author: Janice P. Nimura

item.title: ‘Where the Music Had to Go,’ by Jim Windolf
item.description: Jim Windolf’s new book, “Where the Music Had to Go,” traces the influence of Dylan on the Beatles and the Beatles on Dylan.
item.author: Francine Prose

item.title: ‘On the Calculation of Volume IV,’ by Solvej Balle
item.description: Solvej Balle’s cult hit series about a woman trapped in a time loop continues with a fourth volume.
item.author: A.O. Scott

item.title: ‘RFK Jr.,’ by Isabel Vincent
item.description: In “RFK Jr.: The Fall and Rise,” a New York Post reporter paints an intimate portrait of the Kennedy scion and cabinet member.
item.author: Nicolas Niarchos

item.title: ‘Korean Messiah,’ by Jonathan Cheng
item.description: A new history by Jonathan Cheng argues that an influx of missionaries in the late 19th century profoundly shaped the ruling Kim family dynasty.
item.author: Bruce Cumings

item.title: ‘Go Gentle,’ by Maria Semple
item.description: “Go Gentle” throws together art heists, sexual assault and a coven of middle-aged divorcées on the Upper West Side.
item.author: Jeff Giles

item.title: ‘See You on the Other Side,’ by Jay McInerney
item.description: Jay McInerney has written about the literary party boy Russell Calloway once a decade since the 1990s. He returns in the Covid novel “See You on the Other Side.”
item.author: Dwight Garner

item.title: ‘The Future Is Peace,’ by Aziz Abu Sarah and Maoz Inon
item.description: Aziz Abu Sarah and Maoz Inon both lost loved ones to the conflict in the Middle East. In “The Future Is Peace,” they look for hope and understanding.
item.author: Fintan O’Toole

item.title: ‘Passport Photo Service,’ by Philip Sharkey
item.description: In 10 minutes or less, this mom-and-pop London institution produced stylish snapshots for some of the world’s biggest stars.
item.author: Erica Ackerberg

item.title: ‘Lázár,’ by Nelio Biedermann
item.description: “Lázár,” by Nelio Biedermann, is a multigenerational novel that spans the collapse of a monarchy, two world wars and a revolution.
item.author: Elvia Wilk

item.title: ‘A Terrible Intimacy,’ by Melvin Patrick Ely
item.description: A new book by the historian Melvin Patrick Ely draws on court records to highlight the complex relationships between enslavers and the enslaved.
item.author: Kate Masur

item.title: ‘EMPIRE OF SKULLS’ by Paul Stob
item.description: In “Empire of Skulls,” Paul Stob explores how a mania took over America.
item.author: Jennifer Wright

item.title: ‘The Monuments of Paris,’ by Violaine Huisman
item.description: After devoting her first novel to her wild mother, Violaine Huisman focuses her second on her father, a man who amassed wealth, love affairs and stories.
item.author: Naomi Huffman

item.title: ‘When Tomorrow Burns,’ by Tae Keller
item.description: Tae Keller’s new novel, “When Tomorrow Burns,” offers reassuring answers to the question, “What do you do when your biggest fear comes true?”
item.author: Craig Morgan Teicher

item.title: ‘The Oracle’s Daughter,’ by Harrison Hill
item.description: In his excellent “The Oracle’s Daughter,” Harrison Hill looks at the people and the questions beyond the headlines.
item.author: Carlene Bauer

item.title: ‘London Falling,’ by Patrick Radden Keefe
item.description: A new book by Patrick Radden Keefe retraces the secret life of a 19-year-old Londoner who fell in with a gangster underworld.
item.author: Jennifer Szalai
